content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 20,
1994
Leonard E.
Ostrow
4975 Topaz Avenue
Rocklin,
CA 95677
Re:
Your Request for
Confirmation of Telephone Advice
Our File No..
1—94—212
Dear Mr.
Ostrow:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act.1/
Since your request is one for general
information,
we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).2/
This letter confirms the telephone advice provided to you on
July 13,
1994, regarding your filing obligations under the Act.
On June 29,
1994,
you faxed a copy of an advertisement that you
placed in the newspaper soliciting individuals to run for the
Rocklin City Council.
You indicated that you are not a candidate
or an officeholder.
In addition,
you indicated that you used your
own money to place the notice in a newspaper.
As we discussed, unless you are attempting to influence the
action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of
a clearly identified candidate or candidates,
or the qualification
or passage of any measure,
you will not qualify as a committee
under the Act and will not incur a reporting obligation.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite SO0
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento. CA
95S04-0S07
•
(916) 322-5660


No.
1—94—212
Page Two
Based on the information you sent to the Commission,
you do
not qualify as an independent expenditure committee at this time.
If you have any questions about this letter, please call me
at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
/‘L
Alice Hughes
Political Reform Consultant

sections.question_synthetic: Does a person who placed a newspaper advertisement soliciting candidates for local office qualify as a committee under the Political Reform Act and have reporting obligations?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The requestor does not qualify as a committee or independent expenditure committee under the Act because the advertisement does not attempt to influence voter action for or against a clearly identified candidate or measure, and therefore no reporting obligation is incurred.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter confirming prior telephone guidance. No formal Q/A sections present; question and conclusion synthesized from letter body. Document explicitly states this is informal assistance under Regulation 18329(c) with no immunity protection.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a person who placed a newspaper advertisement soliciting candidates for local office qualify as a committee under the Political Reform Act and have reporting obligations?

CONCLUSION: The requestor does not qualify as a committee or independent expenditure committee under the Act because the advertisement does not attempt to influence voter action for or against a clearly identified candidate or measure, and therefore no reporting obligation is incurred.

On June 29, 1994, you faxed a copy of an advertisement that you placed in the newspaper soliciting individuals to run for the Rocklin City Council. You indicated that you are not a candidate or an officeholder. In addition, you indicated that you used your own money to place the notice in a newspaper. If you have any questions about this letter, please call me at (916) 322—5662. Sincerely, /‘L Alice Hughes Political Reform Consultant
embedding.summary: FPPC confirms telephone advice provided to Leonard Ostrow regarding whether his newspaper advertisement soliciting Rocklin City Council candidates triggers campaign disclosure filing obligations. The FPPC concludes he does not qualify as a committee since the advertisement does not attempt to influence voters for or against identified candidates or measures.
